steps.dialogue: Last time, you compared fractions like [event:6984d739de862523] [fraction numerator=3 denominator=8]three eighths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=5 denominator=8]five eighths[/fraction]: fractions with [event:69850439081a7f2d] different amounts of same-sized [event:698d39d8e312c898] pieces. Today, we'll compare fractions with pieces that [event:698504e9494a189c] aren't the same size.

Here are two side by side bars, perfect for comparing! Look at these two fractions: [event:6985079b2397383e]  [fraction numerator=4 denominator=5]four fifths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction]. Both bars are partitioned into fifths, that means [event:6985099974b28d20] each bar is cut into five equal parts, so the [event:6985084b9e7bc513] pieces are the [event:6985087265b1857a] same size. We can count[event:698508a29d51c98b]:[event:698508d58a6536d4] four pieces here and [event:698508b1c9016707] two pieces there. Please select the correct symbol to compare [event:698508e9ba42c039] them.

prompt.text: Select the symbol to compare [fraction numerator=4 denominator=5][/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5][/fraction]

step.dialogue: Try looking at the pieces. Are they the same size? When the pieces are the same size, try counting them to compare.

metadata.description: Show numbers above shaded sections: 1, 2 on top bar, 1, 2, 3, 4 on bottom bar

step.dialogue: Both fractions have [event:698b6a25cf21a4ba] fifths, the same size pieces[event:698b6a2fb20c1385]. Count the shaded parts[event:698b6a779be9144c], 2 pieces versus 4 pieces. Which bar has more?

Both [event:698b65ea8af9dbca] bars have fifths, so the pieces are [event:698b6663be879412] the same size. Count the shaded pieces[event:698b66c033e83fe6]: [event:698b6921f890a8e5]4 on the left and two on the right. Since 4 is greater than [event:698b6997026b18ea] 2, [fraction numerator=4 denominator=5]four fifths[/fraction] is [event:698b695e406b8ebd] greater than [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Yes. [event:698a54a1deac0e5e] [fraction numerator=4 denominator=5]Four fifths[/fraction] is [event:698b6890500ab41e] bigger [event:698a54c6754d57c5] because [event:698a563fa0dfab4d] four pieces is more than two pieces. When the [event:698a56ad4e42bc32] pieces are the [event:698a5553d36a9bb4] same size, we can compare [event:698a56dceca4eb9a] fractions just by [event:698a54da1bac28c8] counting the pieces.

steps.dialogue: Look at where [event:69851b10b4a01d52] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]one third[/fraction] and [event:69851b1ba7db863f] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction] land on these number lines. [event:69851b5135b5398d] Both show exactly [event:69851cb9a2fdb3c6] one interval, [event:69851ce329e0cb3f] the numerators match. But notice where each one lands[event:69851da9283fa41f]. One of them is much farther from zero than the other. [event:698a5da79aee801e] Which fraction is greater? Select the correct symbol.

prompt.text: Select the symbol to compare [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]one third[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one third[/fraction].

step.dialogue: Both show 1 piece. But look at how far each one is from zero. Which is farther along?

metadata.description: Show moving arrow from 0 to each number, making the distance comparison clear
Fill in the > symbol and show distance from 0 to each point with moving arrows

step.dialogue: Let's work through this together. When we cut the line into [event:698b76d8cb918112] fewer parts, [event:698b72a906922ea3] each part is bigger. So [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]one third[/fraction] is [event:698b780600c67404] farther along than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction], [event:698b76688b074f6c] which means [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]one third[/fraction] is [event:698b79d6655f4a4d] greater than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: [event:698a5ebdee0a65ce] Right! [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]One third[/fraction] is [event:698a5bba7b362e43] farther along the number line than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction]. Even though [event:698a5c0820ac5f2f] both have one piece, [event:698a5c35cc942915] thirds are bigger pieces than sixths.

steps.dialogue: [event:698a5f15b696d7a4]When [event:69851e90a6678f42] denominators match, we will have [event:69851f6a18bc8f7b] equal-size pieces. But [event:69851fbbdd78c1ab] when [event:69851fe16247e57f] numerators match, we have the same number of [event:698a60289b61c06d] pieces, so we have to look at the [event:698a6046a13e3427] size of the pieces instead. We'll practice comparing both types of fractions.
